title: The Dawn of Everything: A New History of Humanity
awards: Wenjin Book Prize, National Library of China (2025)
J.I. Staley Prize, School for Advanced Research (2025)
excerpt: A sweeping reexamination of human history that challenges the conventional narrative of linear progress from primitivism to civilization, arguing that early human societies were far more diverse, complex, and politically creative than we have been led to believe.
keyIdeas: Early human societies were not confined to small egalitarian bands but included large, complex, decentralized polities
The Agricultural Revolution was not a sudden trap but a slow, uneven process that people consciously chose and sometimes rejected
Indigenous critiques of European society during the colonial encounter shaped Enlightenment thinking more than is acknowledged
The three basic forms of social freedom—to move, to disobey, and to reorganize society—were once common and have been progressively lost
The state has no single origin; sovereignty, bureaucracy, and politics are distinct sources of domination that can be disentangled
Many early cities were built without kings, temples, or centralized administration, demonstrating that urbanism and egalitarianism are compatible
keyTakeaways: The standard story of human history—from bands to tribes to chiefdoms to states—is an artifact of 18th-century philosophical debates, not a reflection of archaeological evidence
Agriculture did not inevitably lead to private property, hierarchy, or patriarchy; those outcomes were contingent political choices
Prehistoric peoples experimented with seasonal political regimes, switching between authoritarian and communal systems
The question 'what are the origins of inequality?' is itself ideologically loaded; a better question is how we lost our freedoms
Modern states owe more to colonial violence than to any natural evolutionary progression
Human history is full of playful, hopeful possibilities that dominant narratives have erased
whoShouldRead: Readers of Sapiens, Guns Germs and Steel, or The Better Angels of Our Nature who want a rigorous counter-perspective
Anyone interested in anarchist political theory, indigenous studies, or the history of the Enlightenment
Archaeologists and anthropologists looking for a provocative synthesis of recent research
Activists and organizers seeking historical evidence that alternative social arrangements are possible
General readers tired of deterministic 'big history' and hungry for a more open-ended account of the human past
whoShouldSkip: Readers looking for a concise, linear narrative rather than a sprawling 500-page argument
Anyone who prefers their history free of explicit political commitments and anarchist framing
Those seeking a purely materialist or quantitative analysis of prehistory
Readers easily frustrated by books that spend as much time critiquing other historians as presenting new evidence
